schema.name: TZPID source truth generated from canonical equation master

identity.canonical_title: How to Use This in TZP Modeling and Experiments You Can Run

theory.canonical_statement: ## How to use this in TZP modeling and experiments you can run 1. **Extract angular spectrum from a real image** - Use the same annulus averaging code (I used annulus mean) to extract \(A(\phi)\), then FFT to get \(A_m\). Compare peaks to expected values for aperture geometry (e.g., 6 vanes → m=12). 2. **Recover intrinsic spherical-harmonic content (deconvolution)** - In spectral space, the observed coefficients equal the intrinsic coefficients times the PSF transfer at each m: \(a^{\rm obs}_m = T_m a^{\rm true}_m\). Estimate \(T_m\) from your aperture/PSF model and invert (regularize) to recover \(a^{\rm true}_m\). That’s the recipe I sketched earlier. 3. **Build TZP maps filtered by instrument** - Generate synthetic TZP spherical-harmonic maps (superpositions of Y_l^m), compute their equatorial angular spectra, multiply by PSF spectra, and compare to the real photos. Tune TZP coefficients to best match — that’s a parameter estimation problem. 4. **Measure branching metric** - Compute \(M = |A_{m^*}|^2/\sum_{m\ne m^*}|A_m|^2\). Low M indicates branching/mode-mixing (fractal/IFS); high M indicates a clean aperture spike. Use M as a TZP “bifurcation index.”
theory.technical_interpretation: How to Use This in TZP Modeling and Experiments You Can Run is a series / summation, written M = |A_m^*|^2/∑_mne m^*|A_m|^2. It is expressed as a sum or series over modes or indices, superposing discrete contributions. It is built from A_m^*, m, A_m, defining M. Within the Trawin Zero Point registry it serves as one element of the framework's mathematical narrative.

formation.note: Introduced in the derivation where low M indicates branching/mode-mixing (fractal/IFS); high M indicates a clean aperture spike. Use M as a TZP “bifurcation index.”
